s: Booksellers Mosque — two mosques built eleven years apart
w: Abd al-Mu'min built the first Kutubiyya in 1147, then realised its qibla was off and razed it to build a second, identical mosque eleven years later. Both outlines still coexist in the esplanade: the ruins of the original sit open to the sky, unmarked, a few steps north of the standing mosque. The minaret, at 77 metres, likely inspired the Giralda in Seville. Kutubiyya means 'of the booksellers': at the mosque's base, a hundred stalls once traded manuscripts. The two mosques.
_facts: First mosque built 1147 by Abd al-Mu'min after conquering Marrakesh from the Almoravids
Second mosque rebuilt c.1158 due to perceived qibla misalignment; first mosque's ruins still visible in esplanade
Minaret is 77 metres tall; inspired the Giralda in Seville and the Hassan Tower in Rabat
Name derives from Arabic kutubiyyin (booksellers) — up to 100 book vendors once worked at its base
The ruins of the first mosque outline are preserved in the esplanade north of the standing building

s: Royal mosque, gunpowder blast, and the oath that deposed a sultan
w: Yaqub al-Mansur raised this mosque for his new royal kasbah in 1185-90, positioning it so the caliph could walk directly from palace to prayer. A nearby gunpowder store exploded sometime between 1562 and 1574, badly damaging the southern wing — the long cracks in the minaret remained visible into the 20th century. On 16 August 1907, three days after the French bombarded Casablanca, Madani El Glaoui gathered here with tribal leaders to swear allegiance to Abdelhafid over his brother and trigger the Hafidiya revolt. The Saadian Tombs are immediately south.
_facts: Built 1185-1190 CE by Almohad caliph Yaqub al-Mansur as congregational mosque of the royal kasbah
A nearby gunpowder explosion (1562-1574) severely damaged the mosque; cracks in the minaret were visible until the 20th century
On 16 August 1907, Madani El Glaoui led a gathering here swearing allegiance to Abdelhafid, triggering the Hafidiya revolt against French influence
The Saadian Tombs developed in the cemetery immediately south of the mosque
The minaret's decorative style became the prototype for many subsequent Maghrebi minarets

s: The cobalt garden that saved itself by seducing a couturier
w: Jacques Majorelle, son of the Nancy cabinet-maker Louis, arrived in Marrakech in 1917 to convalesce and never really left. Over forty years he assembled this one-hectare garden around a Cubist villa, painting it all in the dense cobalt blue he had seen on Berber burnouses and tiled kasbahs — a shade he patented as Majorelle Blue. He opened it to the public in 1947 to fund its upkeep; it fell into neglect after his death in 1962. Yves Saint Laurent and Pierre Berge found it in the 1980s, restored it, and scattered Saint Laurent's ashes here in 2008. Now inside: the Berber Museum (Amazigh objects, jewellery, carpets), a 5,000-volume research library with 12th-century Andalusian texts.
_facts: Created by French Orientalist painter Jacques Majorelle (1886-1962) over almost 40 years from 1923
The distinctive cobalt blue Majorelle patented as 'Majorelle Blue', inspired by Moroccan tiles and Berber burnouses
Majorelle opened the garden to the public in 1947 with admission fees to fund maintenance
Yves Saint Laurent and Pierre Berge purchased and restored the garden in the 1980s; YSL's ashes scattered here in 2008
The villa now houses the Musee Pierre Berge des Arts Berberes (Berber Museum) with Amazigh cultural objects

s: Saint-Laurent's Morocco love letter, cubed in terracotta
w: Two museums honour Yves Saint Laurent: one at the Paris headquarters, and this one in Marrakech — because, as Pierre Berge put it, YSL 'owes so much to Morocco, even in the colours and forms of his clothing.' Studio KO built it in 2017 using earth bricks made from Moroccan soil, the facade's latticed brickwork evoking woven fabric. Inside: 7,000 garments, 30,000 accessories, Saint Laurent's sketches and photos, plus a dedicated Jacques Majorelle hall. Wallpaper named it Best New Public Building of 2018. The research library holds 5,000 volumes, including 12th-century Andalusian manuscripts.
_facts: Opened October 2017, financed in part by an auction of Moroccan works of art organised by Pierre Berge in September 2015
Designed by Studio KO; built with earth bricks from Moroccan soil; facade's brick lattice evokes woven textile
Holds 7,000 garments and over 30,000 accessories from Saint Laurent's personal collections
Won Wallpaper magazine's Best New Public Building award at the 2018 Design Awards
Research library includes 5,000 volumes, including Andalusian works dating to the 12th century and books on Amazigh art

n: Zaouia of Sidi Mohammed Ben Slimane al-Jazuli
s: Author of Islam's most-copied prayer book, buried here since 1523
w: Al-Jazuli died near Essaouira in 1465 and was buried in a village called Afoughal. The early Saadians, also from the Sous region, claimed spiritual kinship with his movement, and in 1523-24 Ahmad al-'Araj had the body transferred here and a zawiya built around it. Al-Jazuli's Dala'il al-Khayrat — a collection of prayers for the Prophet divided into seven sections for each day of the week — became the most widely circulated devotional text in the Islamic world after the Quran itself. He is the second of the Seven Saints of Marrakesh. The small adjacent cemetery was the Saadian dynasty's first burial ground, before the Saadian Tombs were built.
_facts: Complex established 1523-24 when Saadian ruler Ahmad al-'Araj transferred al-Jazuli's body from Afoughal (near Essaouira)
Al-Jazuli (d. 1465) compiled the Dala'il al-Khayrat, possibly the most widely circulated Islamic prayer book after the Quran
The Dala'il al-Khayrat is divided into 7 sections, one for each day of the week, for daily recitation
Al-Jazuli is one of the Seven Saints of Marrakesh, central to the city's annual week-long ziyara (pilgrimage circuit)
The cemetery beside the complex served as the first Saadian dynasty burial ground before the Saadian Tombs were built in the Kasbah

s: A Saadian Friday mosque built over a Jewish cemetery
w: In 1557-58 the Saadian sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib decreed that the city's Jews relocate to a new mellah by the Kasbah, freeing their old neighbourhood for redevelopment. The Mouassine Mosque — built 1562-1573 on the site of their former cemetery — formed the centrepiece of a grand civic complex: madrasa, library, hammam, public fountain and animal drinking trough, an arrangement influenced by Mamluk Egypt. Some Jewish residents avoided walking near it for fear of treading over burial ground, and some accounts claim Muslims shared that discomfort. The minaret is unusually short and plain by Saadian standards.
_facts: Built 1562-63 to 1572-73 CE by Saadian sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib; designed as Friday mosque for a new neighbourhood
Erected on the site of a former Jewish cemetery after the 1557-58 expulsion of Jews to the new Mellah by the Kasbah
Part of an unprecedented civic complex including madrasa, library, hammam, fountain and animal water trough — influenced by Mamluk Egypt
Jewish residents reportedly avoided walking near the mosque for fear of the impure burial ground beneath
The minaret is unusually short and simple compared to other Saadian period minarets

s: Oldest mosque site in Marrakech, founded by the city's co-creator
w: Yusuf ibn Tashfin, the Almoravid emir who completed the founding of Marrakech in 1070, built the first mosque on this site in 1071 and reportedly mixed the mortar with his own hands. His son Ali ibn Yusuf replaced it with a grand mosque costing 60,000 gold dinars, with a minaret 30-40 metres tall, around which the whole early city's souk network organised itself. The Almohads demolished or abandoned it in 1147, deeming its qibla orientation heretical, and built the Kutubiyya instead. The present building dates from a complete reconstruction in 1819-20. Closed to non-Muslims — the adjacent Ben Youssef Madrasa is the accessible counterpart.
_facts: Site of the first mosque in Marrakech, built by city co-founder Yusuf ibn Tashfin in 1071
Ali ibn Yusuf (son of Tashfin) rebuilt it at a cost of nearly 60,000 gold dinars c.1120-1132; the city's souks grew around it
Almohad caliph Abd al-Mu'min abandoned or demolished it in 1147, judging the qibla orientation incorrect; built the Kutubiyya instead
Current building is a complete reconstruction finished 1819-20 under Alaouite sultan Suleiman
The qadi (religious judge) of Ben Youssef Mosque has traditional jurisdiction over all of Marrakesh; not accessible to non-Muslims

s: Skeins of dyed wool dripping from the rafters since the Saadians
w: Tuck down the alley beside the Mouassine Mosque complex and the light drops out: overhead, ropes of freshly dyed wool hang like a wet ceiling — crimson, saffron, indigo — dripping onto the cobblestones below. The dyers' guild has occupied this single street for centuries, vats and all; the trade has barely changed. Wool is soaked, wrung, hoisted. The smell of mordants and natural dyes fills the gap between the houses. Come in the morning when the colours are freshest. One of the few surviving guild-specific craft streets in the medina.
_facts: One of the surviving specialised craft souks in the Marrakech medina, dedicated to wool dyeing (teinture)
Located adjacent to the Mouassine Mosque complex in the heart of the old souks district
The dyeing process uses natural and synthetic dyes; wool is soaked in vats, wrung and hung to dry overhead in the alley
The dyers' alley (Souk Sebbaghine / Souk des Teinturiers) is one of the most photographically distinctive streets in the medina

s: The Pasha's personal hammam — still steaming in the medina
w: Thami El Glaoui, Pasha of Marrakech from 1912 to 1956, built his palace, the Dar el Bacha, on a lavish scale to dazzle Churchill, Chaplin, and Colette. The hammam attached to it — a separate structure facing Avenue Fatima Zohra — served the Pasha's household and guests. It still operates today as a working neighbourhood hammam, with its original tilework and star-pierced marble ceilings intact. The adjacent Dar el Bacha palace became the Museum of Confluences in 2017; the hammam continued without interruption. Cold room, warm room, hot room — the Pasha's order of things.
_facts: Part of the palace complex built by Thami El Glaoui (1879-1956), Pasha of Marrakech under French protectorate
El Glaoui's guests at the Dar el Bacha included Winston Churchill, Colette, Maurice Ravel, and Charlie Chaplin
The hammam is located on Avenue Fatima Zohra, near the Dar el Bacha which was converted to the Museum of Confluences in 2017
Still operates as a functioning traditional neighbourhood hammam with original tilework
El Glaoui conspired with the French to depose Sultan Mohammed V in 1953 before recanting in 1955

s: Second oldest Jewish quarter in Morocco, decreed into being 1558
w: When Sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib moved Marrakech's Jewish community to a new quarter beside the Kasbah in 1558, he created the second oldest mellah in Morocco, after Fez. During the 16th and 17th centuries it was one of the city's main commercial hubs — Christian merchants and foreign ambassadors preferred to lodge here, and the Franciscan mission sheltered within its walls after its monastery was destroyed. At its peak in the late 1940s, some 40,000 people lived in this compact district. Today the population is a few hundred; the tall, narrow houses with iron-railed balconies remain. King Mohamed VI allocated over 20 million dollars for its restoration in 2016.
_facts: Created by decree of Saadian sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib in 1558, making it the second oldest mellah in Morocco after Fez
During 16th-17th centuries one of Marrakech's main commercial quarters; Christian merchants and ambassadors preferred to stay here
After destruction of their monastery mid-17th century, Franciscan missionaries lived in the mellah until end of 18th century
Population peaked at an estimated 40,000 in the late 1940s; Jewish emigration after Israeli independence and Moroccan independence reduced this to about 200 today
King Mohamed VI in 2016 ordered restoration of Jewish heritage street names and allocated over USD 20 million for restoration

s: Iron lanterns hammered out while you watch, since the Saadians
w: The blacksmiths' souk sits one alley back from the more touristed Souk Attarine: a narrow street of open workshops where lanterns, grilles, locks and hinges are forged and beaten by hand. The sound is the tell — the ring of hammer on anvil carries before you see anything. Metalwork has been a medina speciality since at least the Saadian period; the intricate pierced-iron lanterns that light every riad in the city come from workshops like these. Sparks, soot, the smell of hot iron. The hammered copper pots in adjacent stalls suggest a trade that has always bled into its neighbours.
_facts: Souk Haddadine (also Souq El Haddadine) is the traditional blacksmiths' and ironworkers' souk in the Marrakech medina
Located adjacent to the dyers' souk (Souk des Teinturiers) in the central souks area
Metalwork crafts have been produced in Marrakech since at least the Saadian period (16th century)
The souk produces the pierced-iron lanterns characteristic of Moroccan riad architecture
